posts.name: Ofer Refaeli
posts.cooked:
robertparker.com

Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

The ultimate guide for exploring the world of fine wines. The Wine Advocate offers valuable insights, practical recommendations and expert knowledge through the fascinating world of wines.

Interesting article about making the best wine and best wine makers.
I probably have a simpler explanation on the one hand, but maybe it can’t be learned and imitated at all on the other. IMO almost anyone, after some study, can make wine. An acquired ability. Only a master craftsman - artist can consistently produce excellent wine. Innate genius. As in music or any other art - anyone can compose music. But highest-level music (Bach, Beethoven, etc.) only an artist can write. It’s something innate, a gift of genius.

link_counts.title: Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Ofer_Refaeli:

Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

Interesting article about making the best wine and best wine makers.
I probably have a simpler explanation on the one hand, but maybe it can’t be learned and imitated at all on the other. IMO almost anyone, after some study, can make wine. An acquired ability. Only a master craftsman - artist can consistently produce excellent wine. Innate genius. As in music or any other art - anyone can compose music. But highest-level music (Bach, Beethoven, etc.) only an artist can write. It’s something innate, a gift of genius.

I sorta see it this way as well, though will admit that it derives as much from a romanticized vision of winemaking.

William’s thesis, in a nutshell, is far more pragmatic, very process-oriented. His thinking is like comparing the engineer to the architect. I’m in the latter camp, but like any fine piece of architecture, it requires a complete integration of the design arts with engineering principles. So innate talent to produce magical wines also requires a refined and repeatable process. And great terroir…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That’s pretty much the opposite of my thesis! And my thesis was actually informed by speaking to, among others, some great artists and athletes (the sort of people who are generally described as “talented”)
.

posts.name: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Ofer Refaeli
posts.cooked:
A mediocre composer took composition lessons from Bach, as much as he wanted and with all his heart. Do you think he would compose music of the quality of Bach’s works? I’m pretty sure not, although he would improve.
A mediocre winemaker took winemaking lessons from a great winemaker. Would he make wine like Henri Jayer or someone on his level? He would improve but probably not reach Henri Jayer level.

posts.name: Jacob Goldman
posts.cooked:
The best whatevers doing whatever have exactly a process of this form - controlled steps across a workable and managable pipeline where gains compound multiplicatively. It is the intense focus and dedication to the perfection of this process that people mistake for talent.

And because of this - of course one can’t take a week-long course chez Jayer and replicate him. It is the endless optimization combined with fearlessness that yields results.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The use of the word “mediocre” makes this something of a circular argument. I think it’s more interesting to try to make sense of Bach’s greatness.

One definition of talent is “innate ability”. But my working definition for it is increasingly, what excellence looks like from the outside.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: I think a composer is largely the wrong analogy. Composers aspire to create the truly original. A talented instrumentalist would be a more apt analogy - they interpret the material they are given. As a winemaker interprets site, weather, grape, and vinification. Truly great performing artists often have innate talents for processing the material, seeing and expressing connections that most laypersons can’t see themselves, but they are enraptured by the results. But mostly, it requires hard, hard work, careful study and an inquisitive mind to interpret and understand the systems they are given. Not all great artists succeed with different materials. Glenn Gould - a great interpreter of (some) Bach, applied his method to Beethoven with questionable-to-disastrous level results.

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked:
A really interesting article!

As a composer I think most of this is also the case for my field; indeed across all artistic disciplines - any area where one benefits from a holistic perspective really (engineering or physics for example). That said, I do think that the ability to perceive things holistically - to see how all components in a system interact - could perhaps be perceived as a talent (though not a subject-specific one). I have had many composition students over the years that I have perceived as talented, who’s ability I imagine could have been put to good use in other areas, and indeed in many cases they have gone on to become ‘talented’ writers, journalists, and professionals in other areas where holistic thinking comes in handy (and might even be considered a requirement). I do not think that everyone is born with the same capacity for this, and while I am sure it is a skill that can be developed (especially during formative years), I do think some people are perhaps more predisposed to it than others (talented perhaps), while others of course have other skills that come more naturally to them.

I do think that the ability to see how all aspects of a system interrelate with one another is something that a lot of great artists have in common - perhaps that is something that they share with the best winemakers. Though I do also think that, in order to become a great composer, you do need a certain talent for music - again, in my experience some people simply do not have much of this. But perhaps this ‘talent’ is less important than the ability to think holistically, as long as one is willing to put the work in. I have certainly had many students with a good helping of the former who have consistently failed to produce good work simply because they have not put the hours in. Conversely, I have seen plenty of young composers for whom musical skills have not come quite so effortlessly, yet have had a good sense of holistic processes, and have achieved great things, due to hard work and perseverance.

Of course there are many things that come in handy, and from what you write I think we agree that one thing or ability on its own is certainly not sufficient in order to succeed (in either field). I also think there are other factors that come in here; confidence playing a particularly important role (though of course one could argue that confidence is a requirement for the kind of determination that you talk about in your article). And while talent for winemaking or music might not be the most important thing, I do think that someone who has very little talent (or none at all, if there is such a thing) would struggle immensely. So perhaps what is required in order to be a good winemaker, artist, or indeed a physicist, is a holistic set of skills (confidence, perspective, determination, work-ethic, knowledge, perseverance, patience, talent and more?) and the ability to apply these skills collectively, which combined achieve the holistic perspective requ are in winemaking. the marginal gains through quantitative, incremental refinements that I think are as often a requisite for greatness in my field as they are in winemaking.

posts.name: Rhoads Wilson
posts.cooked:
This isn’t really getting at the crux of the article, but you mention one can taste, or detect through testing, a loss of precision in barrel, which should be followed up by sulfiting. Could you possibly expand on what you mean by a loss of precision? I’m also quite curious how much time you’d have as a winemaker to correct little issues at this stage in barrel before they compound to ruin the wine.

I really enjoyed the article. Thanks for writing and sharing it!

posts.name: Alec Schingel
posts.cooked:
I have to say, I was very moved by the article by Mr Kelley. I think it was a very thoughtful meditation on the nature of success and “greatness” viewed through a very in depth study of the world of wine and winemakers. But, it was also very easy to see the parallels and simple adaptable lessons that could be applied to a number of disciplines. I think that overall it is a very valuable thesis that I am still contemplating.

I have a few thoughts that may be obvious but I feel like saying so for the sake of conversation. First, we are spending time discussing this idea of “innate talent”. I don’t mean to put words into Mr. Kelley’s mouth but it should be obvious to most that becoming a winemaker rarely happens completely out of the blue. For most new world winemakers, its something that is doggedly pursued due to some level of attraction to the final product or the process itself. For some it may be the “Lifestyle” but those are rarely winemakers that are celebrated. For those in the old world, it could be a choice or in a higher percentage it is patrimonial and the children grow up in that world. I don’t know any talented winemakers who simply got lost in the woods one day with a bucket, found some grapevines, accidentally made some wine, and unbeknownst to them had some “innate talent” for winemaking. Becoming a winemaker is almost always a conscious choice or it is very deeply part of your culture, which makes it self selecting for those in that field. To me that was an inherent assumption to the thesis in this article but it’s still worth saying.

The other point I would make, is that it’s never really fair to compare an overall thesis of the pursuit of greatness with singular exceptions. Mozart would be one example of this. He was a child prodigy, born in the cradle of classical music, pushed to extreme hard work by his father, only to come out the other side with a continued love of his craft and work ethic to continue it. There are other examples of similar situations. Tiger Woods, Michael Jackson, so on. These are a product of very specific and unique circumstances that are hard to replicate or to apply. They are fascinating studies of greatness and in many ways they still fit into the overall argument the Mr Kelley puts forth. I would argue that they are particularly exceptional due to the circumstances that they happened to find themselves and therefore don’t particularly apply as good examples to prove or disprove the argument.

The two people who came to mind as winemakers were JF Mugnier and David Ramey. I loved the episodes of each on Levi Dalton’s Ill drink to that in which they both discuss many of the same ideas put forth in the article.

Sorry lots of random thoughts, but I found the article very interesting and inspiring.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked:
it is funny you compare tiger woods to mozart. there is a great book called range by david epstein that investigates a similar topic and finds most high level achievers more so resemble roger federer in that they were late to pick a specialization (fed did not pursue competitive tennis until his teens which is considered quite late for the sport). the common misconception is that greatness regularly arises from being some type of child prodigy. that is in fact more the exception than the rule.

overall i think there are some interesting points to william’s article but also there are some rather problematic analogies in my opinion. there is so much more latitude in the expression of art than in wine. perhaps natural winemakers push the boundaries of what is a valid expression the most, but not many would claim those are the great wine makers of the world. however, in art moving the needle means changing the paradigm. incremental progress would not have gotten pollock to his drip paintings, or duchamp to the fountain, or even warhol to the silk screen. the list goes on and on. art is more about emergence and shattering the notions of what has been. not to mention, art can still exist outside of its own market. how many artists were forgotten or lost to history only to be later rediscovered and appreciated for their significant contributions to the canon. hilma af klint comes to mind in addition to the obvious dutchman. wine, while still a sensory experience, has very different aims and intents than art with a capital A. if wine maker x produced a wine that was indistinguishable from leroy, drc, rousseau, etc they would be deemed as a great wine maker and they would likely succeed in the market place. in art (or music) the same instance would not elicit much positive fan fare. replication is not emergence. nor is derivation. in wine it is totally ok to produce things which resemble that which has come before so long as the targeted benchmark was accepted to begin with. lachaux can compare himself to d’auvenay or whatever and the market does not mind because the wines are good in the expected fashion. this would never fly in art, nobody would deem it culturally significant if a contemporary artist mimicked the work of an established blue chip artist. wine is more a craft than a true art, even though it does have aesthetic components.

as far as talent in sports, sports do require some talent usually in the form of genetic gifts such as height. of course there are outliers such as muggsy bogues. sports are also far more quantifiable than wine or art so incremental adjustment and the corresponding progress can be far more precisely measured.

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked: Interesting point. Though as an artist, I have to say that my appreciation of art lies far more in the craft than in novelty. Or rather, novelty without craft is uninteresting to me. Which is why I have little or no interest in the work of many well-regarded people in pop- and conceptional art spheres (Warhall, Koons, Hirst etc). Many people in new music and the fine arts would relate to this (though not all would say so). So perhaps at least within fine art, the sentiment that craft is more important than novelty is largely shared. That said, I do think that the strife for novelty is crucial in art. But is that not the case in winemaking as well, to some degree? Those that are remembered as the great winemakers of the past often stand out as having contributed something new to the discipline, in some way(s) or other. While this might not always be important to most wine-drinkers, I imagine it is to those within the field - perhaps in a similar way to art music and fine art. Where the difference lies might be that artists are freer to completely redefine the field, as they have more scope to (re-)shape the materials they work with. But in this respect the question of novelty is perhaps more one of scope than of significance?

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: I see the winemaker as more architect than artist.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Following the tangent: as a winemaker during élevage, you are trying to add as little SO2 (antiseptic / antioxidant / anti-oxidase) as possible, as late as possible. The longer you can defer those additions, the more stable the wine seems to be, and the freer its expression. But, that is fraught with risks: principally from yeast (mainly brettanomyces) and bacteria. It is often rewarding to take risks, but it is important to take them intelligently: it is always better to realize 90% reward than to push for 100% and end up loosing 30%; perfect is sometimes the enemy of (very) good. Brettanomyces can be monitored regularly, and their production of phenols impeded by low cellar temperatures, so that is actually quite manageable. Bacteria, however, can be more risky, and they love the conditions that brettanomyces don’t like. So the moment you perceive anything mousey/funky/slimey in a wine, it’s important to intervene very quickly.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I don’t think I drew too many analogies with the contemporary visual arts, but I’m happy to pursue the comparison.

Art can be produced by two fundamentally different categories of people: artisans and artists. Winemakers are, to me, clearly artisans: this is the world of learned crafts, agglomerated incremental gains, iteration and an obsessive, creative love. Most art, for most of recorded history, was made by artisans, not “artists”. Artists, as opposed to artisans, arrived on the scene relatively recently (turn of the 20th century), and the cultural history of it would be interesting to get in to, in particular with regards to the cult of “originality” as defined as something totally new as opposed to something reinterpreted.

To you, the artists you cite are paradigm shifting; but to me, Pollock made a mess, Duchamp was a practical joker, and Warhol self-consciously trivial. More power to them for having made a lot of money. But they were not engaged in a pursuit that would have been experientially and motivationally recognizable to Botticelli or Turner. And I am just fine if wine has nothing in common with that sort of art.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Novelty comes at you fast in wine, even if you’re not looking for it. Trying to produce e.g. Coche 1996s today is equivalent to saying, try to copy a Piero della Francesca, but using acrylics.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: BTW, thanks everyone for the great discussion - a reminder of why this forum remains so engaging!

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: So, Turley Zinfandel is like an Andy Warhol? Interesting…the colors are both bright indeed!

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
In a completely unrelated field I saw too many quality improvement fads come and go. All sorts of things were tried, and there was never any guiding cultural foundation. So after a few months or a year, when the desired immediate and significant change had not been accomplished things were scrapped and a new, wildly different plan emerged. Lather, rinse, repeat.

Meanwhile, in the same company, the E,H,&S executive created a good plan, and worked diligently to develop and sustain a culture around E,H,&S improvement. When things didn’t go quite as well as desired she engaged the teams to tweak the program, rather than scrap it and start over.

Guess which one worked.

(And no, this wasn’t at Boeing.)

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: firstly, most philosophers in the realm of aesthetics make a distinction that craft is not art. i used examples which would be widely known, not necessarily my own views of high artistic merit. i guess my point is that wine is an anachronism and proudly backward looking to a certain extent. artisans existed under quite different economic conditions. i just don’t see wine as being that precious frankly. even great wine is produced in high quantities compared to works of most widely accepted artisans (such as an independent watch maker, etc).

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked:
Artisanal work may be proudly backward-looking (or rather rooted in an informed historical perspective), but then so is a lot of art. A lot of contemporary art - not just historical pastiche (which I think is equally uninteresting to novelty for novelty’s sake) - is equally rooted in knowledge of the history of the discipline and experience of its techniques. And there are plenty of people in aesthetics who - for better or for worse - see art and craft as symbiotic or even analogous (e.g. art as craft of emotive design, which I personally take issue with, but never mind).

And while winemakers might make more bottles than a watchmaker makes watches, wine is - much like music, though in contrast with most fine arts - ephemeral. Also, fewer people can enjoy it at one time than most works of art. While I do not think this is a good thing per se (of course it would be fantastic if everyone who wanted to could enjoy great wine) I do think that the medium means that these things cannot really be compared. Moreover, while a winemaker may bottle the fruits of their and the vineyard’s labours in numerous bottles, is that the same as each bottle being a separate work? Or are they just individual components of the macro-work, which each imbiber may only enjoy a fraction of at a time? I’m not sure if I’m convinced by that myself, or what it necessarily means, if anything, but it’s interesting to ponder over.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked:
those are fair points but wine is largely determined by the quality of its inputs.

i think collingwood makes some interesting points on all this for those who are interested:
Collingwood’s Aesthetics (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Following the tangent: as a winemaker during élevage, you are trying to add as little SO2 (antiseptic / antioxidant / anti-oxidase) as possible, as late as possible. The longer you can defer those additions, the more stable the wine seems to be, and the freer its expression. But, that is fraught with risks: principally from yeast (mainly brettanomyces) and bacteria. It is often rewarding to take risks, but it is important to take them intelligently: it is always better to realize 90% reward than to push for 100% and end up loosing 30%; perfect is sometimes the enemy of (very) good. Brettanomyces can be monitored regularly, and their production of phenols impeded by low cellar temperatures, so that is actually quite manageable. Bacteria, however, can be more risky, and they love the conditions that brettanomyces don’t like. So the moment you perceive anything mousey/funky/slimey in a wine, it’s important to intervene very quickly.

These are certainly interesting concepts - but are they based on ‘facts’ or based on ‘your experiences’? The idea that the later and less SO2 you add, the ‘freer its expression’ may very well be difficult to actually ‘prove’, right?

Curious to hear your thoughts on this.

Cheers

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
And just like with art and music, there are winemakers who are truly ‘artists’ and ‘craftsman’ and many who are more ‘commerce’ generated. There are many who have studied their craft at a university and more that have not - same as musicians, artists, and chefs for instance.

Love the discussion - now I should read the article

Cheers

posts.name: Jacob Goldman
posts.cooked: Quantity is surely a red herring in this discussion. Wine expresses human interaction and struggle with an environment of immense complexity and later a chemical procedure which clearly also allows for many possible decisions. The fact more than a single grapevine is dealt with cannot meaningfully change the ‘artisan-ness’ of the procedure.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: i simply meant that scaling up does not necessarily diminish quality. look at large champagne houses, bordeaux, etc. they are still considered world class wines and do not fit into the idea of artisan produced.

posts.name: Jacob Goldman
posts.cooked: Fair, but that still revolves around the same latent issue - just because a process can be industrialized at the highest level does not mean that the singular craftsman cannot be a full-blooded artisan.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: This reminds me of organic chemistry lab. Clearly, there is an art to it, but at the same time it is completely process oriented. Each step needs to be clean and perfect and this enables the next step. Waver from one step, and you may end up with problems 3 steps downstream wondering what the heck happened and where. Lots of things are like this. Drywall, cooking, surgery, etc…

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

perfect is sometimes the enemy of (very) good.

Another way to state that is don’t F with it so long that you F it up. One of my favorite professors once told me “Don’t gild the Lily.” Having never heard the expression, I recall asking, “What does that mean?” Gosh, I was so stupid - of course, I am a bit of concrete thinker - when asked “What does it mean when someone says “People in glass houses shouldn’t throw stones.”?” I usually reply because it will break the glass. Yeah, I always fail mental status exams.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
A fun article, which seems very consistent with William’s many posts here over the years.

A few thoughts come to my mind while reading it:

  • I loved William’s notes on a vertical of JMT Bouley Fremiers, where he detailed how there were iterative changes made every year since 2002 to make the wine what it is today. And it reminds me that it was only in ?2017? I think Thomas said the vineyard got to where he wanted it to always be. 15 years of hard work… and seemingly slow iteration… slow “compounding”. For some of these one-person Domaines, its sheer artistry. Fabio in PIedmonte comes to mind in this same vein.
  • It brings the genius of someone like Arnoux to light. Now, I haven’t tasted the most recent Arnoux vintages. But I’ve read how he has made some risky changes year-to-year, without seeming missing a beat on wine quality - and as William’s article notes that can break up years of compounding and reset to zero (like buying a biotech stock that goes to zero). I can see why Arnoux has hype - it’s another type of talent. To take those risks with so many people watching you, takes skill & cojones.
  • On the other hand, I’ve wondered how many winemakers stumble into a formula that ends up commercially successful to their surprise. For example, many on this board feted upon the sweatiest Levet wines… which we now know came from a unkempt cellar… as the winemaker cleaned it up and the wines are now cleaner… what was Levet thinking when people were praising the funk years ago, I wonder? The first time I tried Clape, I was blown away with the briney oliveness. At the time, I thought it was weird that people were applauding it… and wondered if one of the winemakers of the estate years ago thought the same thing… (today, I remember that wine fondly and want to try the heralded 2019, and get smarter on Clape).

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Article was a good read, and I think probably accurate. It’s clear that some people have innate or special talents as musicians, singers, artists, probably the maths and sciences. Much of that can be taught/learned, but that innate ability surely separates the best from the rest. I’m not sure what about winegrowing could fall into that category. It seems like almost anyone could achieve the highest plane of winemaking skills given the desire, and reasonable abilities.

What I missed in William’s article was any reference to the source of the wine, i.e., vineyard quality.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Interesting. I had not seen that, and have yet to experience it.

I have postulated something which is somewhat aligned with that thesis - that, as prices rise, climate changes, and growers of what may have formerly been economically lesser vineyards are able to invest in more intensive vineyard work, some of those historically “lesser” vineyards will undergo a significant rise in quality. Whether any can ultimately equal or surpass what we have until now considered the “best” vineyards, time will tell.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
JBierly:

One of my favorite professors once told me “Don’t gild the Lily.” Having never heard the expression, I recall asking, “What does that mean?” Gosh, I was so stupid

I dislike this idiom precisely because it turns a beautiful piece of Shakespeare into a few words that make no sense.

“To gild refined gold, to paint the lily,
To throw a perfume on the violet,
To smooth the ice, or add another hue
Unto the rainbow, or with taper-light
To seek the beauteous eye of heaven to garnish,
Is wasteful and ridiculous excess”

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
What I missed in William’s article was any reference to the source of the wine, i.e., vineyard quality.

While the examples given focus on winemaking practices, for me, farming is part of the production process, and so the same rules apply. The differences are primarily the longer time-scale of experiments (the lifespan of a yeast being measured in days, vs decades for a vine) and the notable variability between growing seasons (if winemaking requires adaptation every year, viticulture requires a whole lot more).

posts.name: Bill Kimzey
posts.cooked: How much does ability to taste matter? To smell? I think there are innate genetic differences there. We might consider it a talent. Maybe it is analogous to perfect pitch in music. I’m a hobbyist, but I’ve heard/read that there are many steps in the winemaking process where it boils down to taste. Harvest. Are the grapes “perfectly” ripe for what you are doing downstream in the cellar? There has to be variation among winemakers on ability to taste.
